coredata.dc:title: Selective probes for ferric ion: A highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Liping
Wang, Peng
Ren, Miao
Liao, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is gaining more and more attention due to the possibility of its direct use in various fields of science and industry such as biomedical imaging, diagnostics and so on. In the paper, we successfully synthesized a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with urea and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials by a facile and one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared N-CQDs was first used as fluorescent probe for the detection of iron(III) ion (Fe3+) and showed high selectivity and sensitivity. The detection mechanism of iron(III) ion (Fe3+) is further discussed. We assign the Fe3+ selectivity to the special coordinate interaction between Fe3+ and O,N of amide bond on the surface of N-CQDs. The detection limit was 39 nM. It showed excellent linear relationships in wide range from 8 × 10−7 to 2.6 × 10−6 M. The as-synthesized N-CQDs had been also successfully applied for cell imaging and detected Fe3+ in biosystem. It gave positive results when tested in both aqueous solution and living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CQDs
Fluorescence probe
Ferric ion detection
One-step hydrothermal method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence determination of Fe3+ with N-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Laboratory of Jiangsu Province for Chemical Pollution Control and Resources Reuse

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Talent Research Fund Project ( 17YCO25 ) and the Open Project Fund of the Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (contract grant number CSPC2015-6-1 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of N-CQDs. (b) HRTEM image of N-CQDs. (c) Correspoding size distribution of N-CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence Emission Spectra of N-CQDs after adding different concentrations of Fe3 + (0 to 50 μM). (b) FL intensity change (F/F0) on the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of (0–50) μM. (c) The linear plots between the F0/F-1 and Fe3+ concentration (0.8 to 2.6 μM). N-CQDs: 340 mg/L;λex = 566 nm. (d-e) Photographs of N-CQDs aqueous solution under sunlight and UV light.

Fluorescence images of HeLa cells: HeLa cells after incubation with (a) 50 μg/mL N-CQDs, (c) 50 μg/mL N-CQDs and 50 μM Fe3+ for 30 min at 37 °C. The bright–field images (b, d) of HeLa cells corresponding to the fluorescence images (a, c) of HeLa cells.

head.ce:title: Selective probes for ferric ion: A highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Industry, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Industry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is gaining more and more attention due to the possibility of its direct use in various fields of science and industry such as biomedical imaging, diagnostics and so on. In the paper, we successfully synthesized a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with urea and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials by a facile and one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared N-CQDs was first used as fluorescent probe for the detection of iron(III) ion (Fe3+) and showed high selectivity and sensitivity. The detection mechanism of iron(III) ion (Fe3+) is further discussed. We assign the Fe3+ selectivity to the special coordinate interaction between Fe3+ and O,N of amide bond on the surface of N-CQDs. The detection limit was 39 nM. It showed excellent linear relationships in wide range from 8 × 10−7 to 2.6 × 10−6 M. The as-synthesized N-CQDs had been also successfully applied for cell imaging and detected Fe3+ in biosystem. It gave positive results when tested in both aqueous solution and living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: The field of chemical sensing has always depended on novel materials, such as Polymers, nanomaterials and crystals [1]. As nanomaterials, organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots are employed in biological application. However, semiconductor quantum dots may lead to potential cytotoxicity because of heavy metal ions, and organic dyes have poor lightfastness [2]. Therefore fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials include carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [4–7], carbon nanotubes [8], and fluorescent graphene [9], which have drawn increasing attention in recent years due to unique advantages such as biocompatibility, chemical stability, high optical absorptivity and low toxicity [3]. The fluorescent carbon materials have superior properties than traditional fluorescent materials, and their applications have been broadened, such as medical diagnosis [10], photocatalysis [11], photovoltaic devices [12], bioimaging [2], and Biosensing [13]. So far, a number methods for preparing CQDs have been developed, such as arc discharge [14], hydrothermal method [15–19], laser irradiation [20], chemical oxidation [21], ultrasonic treatment [22], electrochemical synthesis [23], and microwave synthesis [24]. In recent years, hydrothermal method has been favored by researchers due to its simple operation, low cost and small environmental pollution [18].

Recently, due to its importance in the environmental and biological concern, metal ions have attracted the attention of researchers [25]. Such as Iron, it plays important roles in both biological and environmental media [26], as an important part of the heme group and the iron sulfur clusters, iron is an important element for various enzymatic reactions and for electron transfer in the respiratory chain [27]. Therefore, many methods have been proposed, such as spectrophotometry [28], cathodic striping voltammetry [29], atomic absorption spectrometry [30], ion chromatography [31] and fluorescence spectrometry [27] used for the determination of iron in natural waters and biological systems [1,2,27,32]. Most of these methods are not only gruelling and time-consuming, but also require sample pretreatment procedures and intricate instrumentation [33]. Fluorescence analysis has distinct predominance, such as simplicity, nondestructive properties and high sensitivity. It has been widespread used in the detection of metal ions [4,6,34–36]. In addition, chemical sensors based on carbon quantum dots for detecting Fe3+ have also been reported. Qu and co-workers synthesized carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine. The surface of the prepared carbon quantum dots was rich in hydroquinone groups, it can be oxidized to the quinone species by iron and resulted in the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots quenching. Therefore, a sensitive trivalent iron chemical sensor was prepared with a detection limit of 0.32 μM [37]. Liu and co-workers synthesized carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal treatment of alginic acid and ethanediamine. At the same time, the fabricated carbon quantum dots could be used to detect Fe3+ with the detect limitation of 10.9 μM [38]. There were also several other carbon quantum dots chemical sensors used for Fe3+ detection reported. The carbon quantum dots prepared by using b-cyclodextrin, the mixture of isoleucine and citric acid, garlic as carbon precursors [39–41]. Thus, we proposed a fluorimetric method based on nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots to detect Fe3 +.

In this work, the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was successfully synthesized by a facile and one-step hydrothermal method with urea and o-phenylenediamine. The prepared N-CQDs exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence, and the fluorescence of N-CQDs can be quenched by Fe3+. In the range of 0 to 50 μM, the PL intensity of the N-CQDs was decreased dramatically with the increase of concentrations of Fe3+. The detection limit was 39 nM at room temperature. We also investigated the effects of Fe3+ on the PL of N-CQDs in cellular environment. The results demonstrate that N-CQDs can be applied for detecting Fe3+ in biosystem.

O-phenylenediamine aqueous solution was added into Urea aqueous solution, and next (NH4)2S2O8 aqueous solution was added to the above mixed solution. Then transferred the mixed solution into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h, and cooled naturally to room temperature. The products was separated and purified by centrifugation and dialysis. The detailed experimental section was mentioned in the supporting information.

Fig. 1a and c showed the magnification TEM image and particle size distribution histogram of N-CQDs, respectively. The HRTEM of Fig. 1b showed that the lattice spacing in the crystalline structure of N-CQD was 0.205 nm, it was close to the (103) diffraction plane of the diamond-like (sp3) carbon and matched the (102) diffraction plane of the sp2 graphitic carbon [48]. The result of Fig. 1b showed that the diameter of the N-CQDs is uniformly distributed from 0.9 to 5.1 nm and an average diameter is 3.04 nm.

Further inspection of the spectra was shown in Fig. S2 (a-e). It could be due to the n-π* transition of N in the aromatic ring, resulting in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CQD showed an absorption peak at 413 nm (Fig. S2e) [42]. The optimum excitation wavelength of the N-CQDs was approximately 420 nm and the optimum emission wavelength of the N-CQDs was 566 nm (Fig. S2a). As shown in Fig. S2b, the emission wavelength shifted 1-3 nm when the excitation wavelength increased from 370 to 480 nm. It showed that the prepared N-CODs had good monodispersity in fluorescence intensity and size, and obviously different from other CQDs for analytical application [48]. Meanwhile, the emission intensity reached a maximum when excited at 420 nm. UV–vis and fluorescence spectra reflected the optical properties of N-CQDs. The fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs was also measured. As shown in Fig. S2c, the fluorescence decay for N-CQDs was double-exponential with the weighted-average lifetime of 2.75 ns. The long decay component was 4.59 ns (42.03%), and the short decay component was 1.41 ns (57.97%). Moreover in Fig. S2d, the fluorescence decay for N-CQDs with Fe3+ was double-exponential with the weighted-average lifetime of 2.82 ns. The long decay component was 5.06 ns (37.64%), and the short decay component was 1.47 ns (62.36%).There is no energy and charge transfer between Fe3+ and N-CQDs due to the fluorescence lifetime without significant changes, the formation of a stable complex between ferric iron and N-CQDs may be the main cause of fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs.

In addition, the surface groups of the synthesized N-CQDs were also investigated using FT-IR (Fig. S3a) and XPS (Fig. S3c-f). As shown in Fig. S3a, the N-CQDs own characteristic absorption bands of NH and OH stretching vibrations at 3440 cm−1, the CH stretching vibrations at 2930 cm−1and 2850 cm−1. In the FT-IR spectrum, the peak at 1630 cm−1 demonstrated the presence of an amide bond [42], and CN stretching vibrations at 1380 cm−1 in quinoid imine units [43], CH out-of-plane bending vibration characteristic absorption peak at 762 cm−1 [44]. The Raman spectroscopy of the N-CQDs (Fig. S3b) showed two closely peaks at 1356 cm−1 (D band) and 1531 cm−1 (G band) of carbon respectively [45]. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S3f) of N-CQDs showed a C 1 s peak at ca. 284.54 eV, an O 1 s peak at ca. 525.85 eV and a N 1 s peak at ca. 398.59 eV. The elemental composition of N-CQDs were C 69.48%, N 27.08% and O 3.44%, respectively. Fig. S3c showed the O 1 s XPS spectrum of N-CQDs. It was mainly divided into two peaks, corresponding to signals of CO (531.54 eV) and COH (534.2 eV). N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. S3d) showed that there were also two peaks, which indicated the existence of CN (398.57 eV) and NH (400.3 eV) bonding models in N-CQDs. The C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. S3e) demonstated that the existence of CO (288.2 eV), CO (286.3 eV), CN (285.03 eV), and CC (284.01 eV) bonding models in N-CQDs [46–48]. Therefore, we could conclude that the as-prepared N-CQDs have abundant amide structure, slight hydroxyl on their surface.

As it is well known that the states of the particle surface affected the optical properties of N-CQDs. Different pH values may have different effects on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity at different pH values was studied. The results (Fig. S4a) indicated that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs increased during the change of PH from 2 to 5. However, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was stable with the PH range of 5 to 12. The phenomenon may be due to the protonation of the surface functional groups on N-CQDs in an acidic environments. Due to the stability of N-CQDs, it could be applied to a variety of biological environments. But in lower pH, the amide bond was easily protonated. In higher pH, the amide bond was easily hydrolyzed, so it could not coordinate with the Fe3+ effectively. As a result, the fluorescence intensity did not change significantly, thus affecting the detection of Fe3+. Fig. S4b showed that fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs did not obviously change under visible light for more than a week. It indicated that N-CQDs still had high stability after continuous light irradiation. Therefore, N-CQDs could be used in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of Fe3+ with N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, selectivity has become an important index to evaluate chemical sensors [49]. In addition, due to its wide application in different fields, the highly selective recognition of metal ions by N-CQDs has attracted extensive attention from researchers [50]. Therefore, high selectivity is the basis for the successful detection of chemical sensors. The ability of N-CQDs to recognize ions in aqueous systems was analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy measurements. The selectivity of N-CQDs was shown in Fig. S4. Except for Fe3+, the addition of 50 μM of other metal ions to the N-CQDs solution did not cause any dramatic changes in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S5a). Furthermore, when other metal ions and three anions (Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −) were present, they also did not affect the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on N-CQDs (Fig. S5b-c). The coordinate interaction between Fe3+ and O, N of amide bond on the surface of N-CQDs is stronger than other metal ions. We speculate that the spatial structure of N-CQDs can accommodate Fe3+. And the presence of Fe3+ caused the N-CQDs aggregate to form larger particles leading to fluorescence quenching. Therefore, N- CQDs had a higher selectivity for Fe3+ [48,53]. The Fig. S1 showed the possible structure of N-CQDs and its fluorescence detection mechanism for Fe3+. In addition, in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the absorption band of the Fe-N-CQDs was at 302 nm, which had a blue-shift (Fig. S2a).

It indicated that the Fe3+ and N-CQDs formed a stable complex, which leaded to fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence quenching was consistent with static quenching. As shown in Fig. 2a, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at 566 nm gradually decreased as the concentration of Fe3+ increased from 0 to 50 μM. Fig. 2b showed fluorescence intensity change (F/F0) on the concentration of Fe3+, which corresponded to Fig. 2a. Thus lead to a color changed from yellow to orange-yellow under sunlight, and a color changed from strong yellow fluorescence to weak white fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 2d-e). The fluorescence quenching of the fluorescence intensity peak at 566 nm followed the Stern-Volmer equation by a static mechanism: [51].
(1)
F 0 / F − 1 = Ksvc
where Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, c is the Fe3+ concentration, and F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs at 566 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. The Stern–Volmer plot shown in Fig. 2c was fitted by a linear Stern-Volmer equation over the concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 8× 10−7 to 2.6 × 10−6 M. The correlation coefficient (r2) and Ksv were 0.9997 and 0.01291, respectively. The detection limit of Fe3+ was much lower than the maximum level (5.4 × 10−6 M) by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) for drinking water [52]. It was also much lower than the detection limit of Fe3+ by previously reported carbon quantum dots-based nanosensors (Table S1). It showed that the N-CQDs sensing system prepared in this paper was much more sensitive to Fe3+ and broadened the study of the fluorescence sensor for Fe3+.

The anti-interference ability of N-CQDs was very important in practical biological imaging application. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs had no obvious change in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of 1.0 M. We investigated whether N-CQDs could be used for cell imaging and detection of intracellular Fe3+. HeLa cells were incubated with N-CQDs (50 μg/mL) in DMEM for 30 min at 37 °C, and followed by addition of Fe3+ (50 μM) for an additional 30 min. Then, the cells were washed with PBS, the cell imaging were taken with a Zeiss LSM 710 confocal microscope. It could be seen from the images that HeLa cells incubated only with N-CQDs showed strong fluorescence, but the fluorescence of HeLa cells became less intense when Fe3+ was added (Fig. 3 ). Therefore demonstrated that N-CQDs could be utilized to detect Fe3+ in living cells.

In short, the novel N-CQDs were successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of urea and o-phenylenediamine. In short, the novel N-CQDs was successfully synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The N-CQDs showed good water solubility and exhibit strong yellow fluorescence. FT-IR and XPS characterization showed that the prepared N-CQD had an amide bond. Selectivity test and detection limit also showed that N-CQDs exhibited higher sensitivity and selective response to Fe3+. These properties showed that N-CQDs are very suitable for the detection of Fe3+ in environmental and biological systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Talent Research Fund Project (17YCO25) and the Open Project Fund of the Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (contract grant number CSPC2015-6-1).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2018.07.045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mater. Sci. Eng. C Mater. Biol. Appl.
